entities.title_ar: Find the Cause of a Full Filesystem
entities.goal_ar: Determine what is consuming disk space.
entities.summary_ar: Identify the affected filesystem, inspect inode usage, and locate large or deleted-open files before removing data.
keywords_ar: Find the Cause of a Full Filesystem
Identify the affected filesystem, inspect inode usage, and locate large or deleted-open files before removing data.
prerequisites_ar: Do not delete data until you understand its purpose.

Review the output and confirm that it matches the intended result.

fixes_ar: Restart the owning service only after verification.

safety_notes_ar: Do not delete a file merely because it is large.

entities.summary_ar: The service manager and initialization system used by RHEL to start the system and manage units.
keywords_ar: A service is one type of systemd unit.
systemctl manages unit state and boot-time enablement.
journalctl reads logs recorded by systemd-journald.
Unit files may be extended or overridden without editing vendor files.
entities.definition_ar: The service manager and initialization system used by RHEL to start the system and manage units.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It explains how services start, why dependencies matter, and where to inspect failures.
key_points_ar: A service is one type of systemd unit.
systemctl manages unit state and boot-time enablement.
journalctl reads logs recorded by systemd-journald.
Unit files may be extended or overridden without editing vendor files.
entities.mental_model_ar: Units describe resources; targets group units; systemd resolves dependencies and activates them.
misconceptions_ar: Starting a service now does not automatically enable it at boot.
Diagnose a failed service before repeatedly restarting it.

entities.summary_ar: A mandatory access-control system that applies security policy in addition to traditional Linux permissions.
keywords_ar: Enforcing mode blocks policy violations and records AVC events.
File contexts identify how policy treats a path.
restorecon restores a path policy-defined context.
entities.definition_ar: A mandatory access-control system that applies security policy in addition to traditional Linux permissions.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It helps distinguish a Unix permission problem from a policy denial and supports safer remediation.
key_points_ar: Enforcing mode blocks policy violations and records AVC events.
File contexts identify how policy treats a path.
Booleans enable documented policy options.
restorecon restores a path policy-defined context.
entities.mental_model_ar: A process domain requests access to a labeled object; policy decides whether that access is allowed.
misconceptions_ar: Disabling SELinux is not a proper long-term fix.
chmod 777 does not resolve an SELinux denial.

entities.summary_ar: The system used to install, update, verify, and remove RPM packages from configured repositories.
keywords_ar: DNF resolves dependencies and repository metadata.
RPM queries the local package database and package files.
Repositories determine which package versions are available.
Transaction history helps audit package changes.
entities.definition_ar: The system used to install, update, verify, and remove RPM packages from configured repositories.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It prevents unmanaged installations and makes updates, verification, and rollback easier to control.
key_points_ar: DNF resolves dependencies and repository metadata.
RPM queries the local package database and package files.
Repositories determine which package versions are available.
Transaction history helps audit package changes.
entities.mental_model_ar: Repositories provide metadata and signed RPM packages; DNF resolves dependencies; RPM records installed files.
misconceptions_ar: Installing a random RPM directly may bypass dependency resolution.
A package name is not always the same as a product name.

entities.summary_ar: A dynamic firewall manager that groups access rules into zones associated with interfaces or source networks.
keywords_ar: Runtime rules apply immediately but do not survive a reload.
Permanent rules survive reloads and reboots.
Services represent documented groups of ports.
The active zone must match the interface or source being diagnosed.
entities.definition_ar: A dynamic firewall manager that groups access rules into zones associated with interfaces or source networks.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It helps you open only the required access in the correct network context.
key_points_ar: Runtime rules apply immediately but do not survive a reload.
Permanent rules survive reloads and reboots.
Services represent documented groups of ports.
The active zone must match the interface or source being diagnosed.
entities.mental_model_ar: Traffic enters through an interface or source, matches a zone, and is evaluated against that zone services and ports.
misconceptions_ar: Opening a port in the wrong zone does not make it reachable.
A firewall rule cannot help if no process is listening.

entities.summary_ar: The traditional access model based on the file owner, group, others, and read, write, and execute bits.
keywords_ar: r, w, and x have different meanings for files and directories.
Ownership and group membership determine which permission class applies.
ACLs can add permissions beyond the basic mode bits.
Every parent directory must allow traversal to reach a path.
entities.definition_ar: The traditional access model based on the file owner, group, others, and read, write, and execute bits.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It lets you grant the minimum access required without weakening the entire system.
key_points_ar: r, w, and x have different meanings for files and directories.
Ownership and group membership determine which permission class applies.
ACLs can add permissions beyond the basic mode bits.
Every parent directory must allow traversal to reach a path.
entities.mental_model_ar: The kernel evaluates the caller identity against ownership, mode bits, ACLs, and other security controls.
misconceptions_ar: chmod 777 is not a safe general solution.
Correct mode bits do not rule out an SELinux denial.

entities.summary_ar: The identity model that maps local accounts to numeric IDs, groups, home directories, shells, and privileges.
keywords_ar: Local users are recorded in /etc/passwd and related databases.
Supplementary groups grant shared access without changing ownership.
The wheel group commonly receives sudo access on RHEL.
Service accounts should receive only the access they require.
entities.definition_ar: The identity model that maps local accounts to numeric IDs, groups, home directories, shells, and privileges.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It is the foundation for authentication, file ownership, sudo delegation, and service accounts.
key_points_ar: Local users are recorded in /etc/passwd and related databases.
Supplementary groups grant shared access without changing ownership.
The wheel group commonly receives sudo access on RHEL.
Service accounts should receive only the access they require.
entities.mental_model_ar: A user has a UID, primary group, optional supplementary groups, and files owned by numeric identities.
misconceptions_ar: Adding a user to a group may require a new login session.
A user name is a label; ownership is stored using numeric IDs.

entities.summary_ar: A troubleshooting model that moves from link state and IP addressing to routing, ports, services, and firewall policy.
keywords_ar: The interface must be up and have a valid address.
A route must exist to the destination.
The application must listen on the intended address and port.
firewalld decides whether the active zone allows access.
entities.definition_ar: A troubleshooting model that moves from link state and IP addressing to routing, ports, services, and firewall policy.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It prevents jumping directly to the application before proving the lower network layers.
key_points_ar: The interface must be up and have a valid address.
A route must exist to the destination.
The application must listen on the intended address and port.
firewalld decides whether the active zone allows access.
entities.mental_model_ar: Interface → IP address → route → listening socket → service → firewall → remote client.
misconceptions_ar: A successful ping does not prove that an application port is open.
An active service may still listen only on localhost.

entities.summary_ar: A standard layout that places configuration, logs, programs, temporary data, and service data in predictable locations.
keywords_ar: /var contains changing data such as logs and caches.
/run contains temporary runtime state for the current boot.
entities.definition_ar: A standard layout that places configuration, logs, programs, temporary data, and service data in predictable locations.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It helps you predict where files belong and distinguish disposable data from critical system data.
key_points_ar: /etc contains system-wide configuration.
/var contains changing data such as logs and caches.
/usr contains programs and shared resources.
/home contains user data.
/run contains temporary runtime state for the current boot.
entities.mental_model_ar: The root directory is a tree, and each major branch has an expected purpose.
misconceptions_ar: A large file is not automatically safe to delete.
/tmp is not a permanent data store.

entities.summary_ar: A layered path from a physical or virtual device through partitions or LVM, a filesystem, and a mount point.
keywords_ar: lsblk displays devices and their basic relationships.
LVM organizes storage into PVs, VGs, and LVs.
A filesystem such as XFS or ext4 lives on a device or logical volume.
mount attaches a filesystem to a directory.
entities.definition_ar: A layered path from a physical or virtual device through partitions or LVM, a filesystem, and a mount point.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It prevents confusing disk expansion, logical-volume expansion, and filesystem expansion.
key_points_ar: lsblk displays devices and their basic relationships.
LVM organizes storage into PVs, VGs, and LVs.
A filesystem such as XFS or ext4 lives on a device or logical volume.
mount attaches a filesystem to a directory.
entities.mental_model_ar: Disk/Partition → PV → VG → LV → Filesystem → Mount point.

entities.summary_ar: A secure protocol in which a client connects to sshd, negotiates encryption, authenticates identities, and opens an encrypted channel.
keywords_ar: The ssh client connects to sshd on the server.
The public key is stored on the server; the private key remains with the client.
The -vvv option shows the stage at which the connection fails.
entities.definition_ar: A secure protocol in which a client connects to sshd, negotiates encryption, authenticates identities, and opens an encrypted channel.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: It shows why a failure may belong to networking, the firewall, sshd, host identity, or user authentication.
key_points_ar: The ssh client connects to sshd on the server.
The public key is stored on the server; the private key remains with the client.
known_hosts verifies the server identity.
The -vvv option shows the stage at which the connection fails.
entities.mental_model_ar: Client → TCP port → sshd → host key → user authentication → shell.

entities.summary_ar: A way to compose small commands so one command output becomes another command input while stdout and stderr can be saved or redirected.
keywords_ar: | sends stdout to the next command.
> replaces a file, while >> appends to it.
2> redirects stderr, and 2>&1 combines it with stdout.
Tools such as grep, sort, and awk become more powerful when composed.
entities.definition_ar: A way to compose small commands so one command output becomes another command input while stdout and stderr can be saved or redirected.
entities.why_it_matters_ar: This composition model is the foundation of efficient shell searching, analysis, and automation.
key_points_ar: | sends stdout to the next command.
> replaces a file, while >> appends to it.
2> redirects stderr, and 2>&1 combines it with stdout.
Tools such as grep, sort, and awk become more powerful when composed.
entities.mental_model_ar: Each command is a small machine: it accepts text, transforms it, and emits text for the next command.

entities.summary_ar: A foundation path covering navigation, files, permissions, packages, services, and logs.
keywords_ar: A foundation path covering navigation, files, permissions, packages, services, and logs.
entities.description_ar: A foundation path covering navigation, files, permissions, packages, services, and logs.

outcomes_ar: Collect evidence before changing the system.
Verify the fix and maintain a rollback plan.

entities.goal_ar: Determine whether SSH is failing at the network, service, or authentication layer.
entities.summary_ar: Trace the failure from network reachability to the SSH port, sshd, host keys, and authentication logs.
keywords_ar: Trace the failure from network reachability to the SSH port, sshd, host keys, and authentication logs.
prerequisites_ar: Alternative server access before changing sshd.

entities.goal_ar: Identify and correct an SELinux denial without disabling SELinux.
entities.summary_ar: Confirm the SELinux mode, inspect AVC events and file contexts, apply a documented correction, and verify the result.
keywords_ar: Confirm the SELinux mode, inspect AVC events and file contexts, apply a documented correction, and verify the result.
prerequisites_ar: A reproducible operation that triggers the denial.

fixes_ar: Use a documented Boolean or port type rather than disabling SELinux.

safety_notes_ar: Do not disable SELinux as a troubleshooting shortcut.
